sentences.text: So I carried on studying and working in Birmingham.
And I'm very proud to have picked up the Brummie dialect very soon.
And I'd like to think that I don't have too much of a German accent anymore, because generally in Birmingham, everybody seems to have accepted me as a Brummie.

And on that basis, I tend to think that I haven't been affected by the Holocaust at all, because I'm just the same as everybody else who worked at BSA Tools or who-- all the friends who did their engineering degree or the friends that I played table tennis with.
I played lots of table tennis at that time, also some football from [INAUDIBLE], et cetera.
And I got my engineering BSE degree with first class honors in 1954.
And I went on to do a master's degree in thermodynamics in 1955 at Birmingham University, the one year that I've actually studied at a university.

I-- I-- I haven't been at schools and universities very often in my life kind of thing, so I very much appreciated the Birmingham University, to see what it was like as a student, as a real student, and all the opportunities.
And I played lots of sports in that year apart from studying as well.
And then I left BSA and eventually transferred to Lucas.
And I worked at Lucas, and Girling in particular, on braking systems for 1958 until 1990, so that's 32 years, initially, on the design and performance of braking systems.

And I traveled a lot to Italy a lot, to Fiat and all that, and later on to Japan and I taught-- helped to teach the Japanese vehicle manufacturers on how to make disk brakes Nowadays, they know it better than we do over here.
But then I had an opportunity to become involved in international standards and regulations.
The man before me had left.
And somebody said, why don't you do that job?
So hesitantly, I accepted in 1970 and I became involved in international standards and regulations for the braking systems of cars and trucks and trailers and buses.
And I went to lots of meetings, national meetings, international meetings.
And I became a world expert on braking regulations.
I've written lots of papers and collected books on-- on the subjects and I've won prizes for my regular presentations in America every year in Detroit for the American motor industry.
I would give an explanation of the European regulations.
And I worked in Brussels for the EEC Commission and I worked in Geneva for United Nations on the-- all of this international braking regulations.
And basically, I became a very big fish in a very small pond.
And on the basis of all that work that I did, in the new year's honors list in 1990, I got an MBE, very much to my surprise and to everybody else's surprise.
There's nobody in my field that ever got anything like that before.
Everybody was quite amazed and so was I.
I thought the MBE should have been for my-- my wife, because she does all the kind of things that you get MBEs for.
Never realized I would get an MBE, but I did.

The interesting thing about the MBE, apart from getting it of course, and the subsequent visit to Buckingham Palace, the interesting thing about the MBE was that local reporters came around to interview me from the and the And they said, how old are you and how long have you lived here and where did you live before, where were you born?
And within five questions, every reporter had found out that I'd been to Belsen.
And they forgot about the MBE and they all wanted to know about Belsen.
And there was this headline, 'MBE for Belsen Survivor' and all these kinds of things in the newspapers.
And one of the photographers that came along, he was a war buff in touch with the [INAUDIBLE].
And he said-- this was 1990, January-- and he said, did you know that there is a big event in Belsen this year, in April, 1990, because it's 45 years after the liberation of Belsen?
It's also Yom HaShoah, Day of the Holocaust.
And it's also the opening of a new documentation center in Belsen in April, 1990.
And I said, no.
I didn't know anything about that.
And he got me all the information and he got me really interested.
And he said, would you like to go?
And we thought about it here, my family, which I better mention as well in a minute.

We thought about it and eventually Ritchie, my brother, and my daughter, Judith, who was 18, 19 at that time and very much interested in Judaism and the Holocaust, the three of us together with a press photographer and an editor from the we went back to Belsen in 1990 for the commemoration ceremony, spent the weekend there.
We met lots of other survivors.
And from-- after I came back here-- it was very interesting, especially for my daughter.
She really was impressed with the whole event.
When we came back, the synagogue-- my synagogue asked, tell us about it.
What was it like?
So we had an evening meeting [INAUDIBLE], and I gave a talk about returning to Belsen.
And everybody thought it was very good.
And it got written up and other people got to hear about it.
And then somebody said, would you mind giving it to my Rotary Club?
So I said, no.
And then I gave the talk to the Rotary Club.
And I expanded the talk from not only return to Belsen, but also Belsen during the war.
And I started giving these talks either about Belsen or Belsen and Buckingham Palace and all those sort of things.
And it grew and grew.
And I started giving the talks to college students and then to schools.
And now, because the Holocaust is part of the curriculum, history curriculum, more and more schools are now inviting survivors to talk about their experiences.

And-- and I go around there and I do it, often on behalf of the Spiral Institute, sometimes on behalf of friends or colleagues, people that I meet, but most often now in conjunction with Stephen Smith from the Beth Shalom Holocaust-- not Museum, Exhibition, Memorial Centre in Laxton near Nottingham, and that started last year.
And I'm very much involved with him, very much appreciate the work that he's done, and very keen to collaborate with him and talk to students.
And he takes me around all over the country to talk to students.
And I would like to think that this return to talking about the Holocaust all happened because of the MBE and the press photographers who took me back to Belsen in 1990.
And it's really only from then that I've started to meet other survivors and heard their stories and really become interested.
I also took early retirement in 1990.
And 1990 was a good year, because I-- I won two major prizes in America and in England for my braking regulations.
I took early retirement.
I went to Buckingham Palace.
I went to Belsen.
Everything happened in 1990.
And from that time onwards, my life has really completely changed now.
And I'm back on the Holocaust trail.
I-- I must say something about my family.
I'm very sorry to have relegated them to last.
But I met my wife Corinne during the '60s.
And She met me 10 years before I met her, or she noticed me 10 years before I noticed her.
And we met at jazz clubs, actually, et cetera.
We got married in-- I must get this right-- 26th of March, 1964.
She was not Jewish, but she was interested in religion.
She came from a family of 11 brothers and sisters, complete football team.
Took me a long time to get hold of all the names.
And-- but she-- her family had not been able to give her a very good education.
She was a nurse and she was also a midwife.
But she hadn't really studied a lot.
And she really got going on the studying.
She took a BA from the Open University and she studied comparative religions.
And on the basis of that and me, she decided to convert to Judaism, which she did in our progressive synagogue, in Sheepcote Street in Birmingham.
And now, as always happens, she is much more of a Jew than I am and she knows much more about Judaism than I am-- than I know.
And she has been every function in the synagogue, including just finished three or four years as chairman of the synagogue.
Next year she's going to be president of the synagogue.
And-- and she is a totally committed Jew.
We have three lovely children, starting with Nicholas who was born in 1965.
And they all went to university and got their degrees.
And Nicholas lives in Birmingham.
He married a couple of years ago, Julie Green.
And only two or three weeks ago-- three weeks ago, they had their first child, our first grandchild, Alexander, who is waiting in the wings to make his entrance later on.
He works in marketing for an advertising company not very far from here, Nicholas.
So I hope he will also be able to come and be on the video later on.
Simon was born two years later, in 1967.
He also got a degree and a master's degree in geophysics.
He is in the oil exploration business, currently working in Holland.
He was here a couple of days ago for Easter, Pesach.
But unfortunately, he's in Holland at the moment and he won't be able to be able to be here.
But we hope to show you some photographs of Simon.
And then there's Judith, the apple of my eye, currently out on two weeks leave from Israel.
She made Aliyah.
As soon as she finished her university degree at Leeds she went on Aliyah and has now lived for 18 months in Israel, first in Haifa, now in Jerusalem.
And she's working for the youth movement, the reform movement in the education side of children.
And she's very much into Zionism, Judaism, Holocaust, et cetera, et cetera, as long as it's not ultra-orthodoxy.
That's the only thing she hates.
And she's here and you'll be able to see her later on.
Well, it is, really.

And the book that I'm writing, I hope you don't pinch the title, is because I feel that it is not a unique achievement, but quite an achievement to come from the depth of Belsen to the peak of Buckingham Palace, in my opinion anyway.
For me it is a tremendous achievement.
I'm-- I'm very proud of it.
I don't know why I got the MBE.
But then, I don't know why I went to Belsen, except for [INAUDIBLE].

So basically, the Buckingham Palace thing was that we first received a letter from the Prime Minister, dropped to a letter box on the 11th of the 11th in 1989, not exactly 11 o'clock, but almost on Armistice Day, Armistice Hour.
And it was addressed to me and it said top secret and all this from Her-- from the Prime Minister's office and all that kind of stuff.
And I couldn't believe it was for me.
I thought it was for Corinne.
Anyway, I opened it and it said that the Prime Minister has it in mind to award the MBE.
If you're agreeable, would you please fill in this form, you know?
So I was very impressed that she was thinking about me, [INAUDIBLE] this in mind, Mrs.
Thatcher.
And I was quite agreeable to receiving it, so I sent it back and said, yes.
We heard nothing else.
That was the 11th of November.
You won't hear anything else now, and you might get it and you might not get it, until the list is published.
So on the day before the list is published, the 30th-- we were not allowed to tell anybody about it, so we didn't tell anybody, especially not our kids.
Corinne was the only one who knew about it.
Very difficult not to tell anybody.
And on the 30th of December then, the press photographers rang up, so I knew I was in the list and we had all these conversations, which led to Belsen kind of thing and then we had the list.
And then we heard nothing again kind of thing.
And eventually later on we had another letter saying there was an investiture.
We didn't understand all these words, had to look it all up, and lots of congratulations from all kinds of people I've never heard of.
And this investiture, and it says, you're commanded to be at Buckingham Palace-- I can't remember the exact words, because I don't talk in those sort of words-- on such a such a day.
And I found out later on that you couldn't write back saying that you had a meeting that day or you were abroad or on holiday, you know.
You were commanded to be there and that was it and you had to be there.
And you were allowed to bring with you-- what it amounted to was one wife of your own and two children of your own.
You were allowed to bring two people or in extraordinary circumstances three, but that was it, one wife and two children.
So I was all right on the one wife, but on the two children, we actually had three children.
So we had a big debate with the children about who did not have to go, because none of them actually wanted to go at first, I think.
[LAUGHS] So Simon was-- he drew the short straw and decided he didn't want to go anyway, so he didn't want to go.
What was much more important was we also had a car parking sticker for Buckingham Palace.
And they all wanted the car parking sticker to have on their cars, because they were all at university and they all had cars.

And they all felt that the carpark sticker with Buckingham Palace on it would-- kind of thing-- be influential with the traffic wardens kind of thing in Solihull, so that was the bit they really wanted.
They weren't interested in going to Buckingham Palace.
Anyway, we did go to Buckingham Palace with Nick and Judith.
And we arranged to meet Simon outside afterwards, but we found out later on Simon could have been our chauffeur and then he would've got in, not into the actual ceremony, but into Buckingham Palace.
When we got into Buckingham Palace, we were-- I won't use the word now, because Corinne goes mad.
We were divided up into those people, the recipients of awards go one way and the family goes the other way.
So Corinne and Nick and Judith went the other way.
And they had a very nice tour of Buckingham Palace.
This is before they had tours of palace as they have nowadays, so it was very difficult to get into Buckingham Palace.
So they had a very nice tour and they eventually finished up in the main ballroom in Buckingham Palace with the guards, bands playing all the while.
And they saw the entire ceremony.
It was the Queen herself.
Sometimes it's the Prince of Wales and other people who do it.
But on this day, it was the Queen herself, in yellow, that did the ceremony herself.
And she actually did 134 awards in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just over one hour.
So it was something like half a minute per award.
And so therefore, it all had to be very highly organized.
And it started with knights.
So Trevor-- Trevor Chinn got his knighthood at that time.
He was one of the first on that investiture.
But I didn't know anything about that.
I was herded in the other direction.
I was all dressed up in morning suit and everything.
And we had to hand in our gloves and our hat, not allowed to take that in.
We had to hand in our camera, not allowed to take that in.
And we were herded off into a certain area.
And every group of people, every award, the knights went here.
The OBEs went there.
The KBEs went somewhere else.

MBEs, of which there were the most of, because it was the lowest thing that the Queen herself does, we finished up in a great big long corridor with the Rubens painting all the way along and we all stood there.
First of all, we were allowed to go to the toilet first, so I went to the toilet.

And of course, I immediately looked for souvenirs, having been well trained, perhaps organizing as we used to call it, which I forgot in our story, and also from my year of student days where we went around the country with table tennis teams.
We were well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well aware of finding souvenirs from various places.
And I immediately looked for anything with Buckingham Palace on it, but there was nothing.
They obviously had people like me there before at the Palace, you see, and there was nothing really worth taking.
And there was nothing to eat or drink.
And in fact, I was-- and Corinne, we were all in Buckingham Palace for the best part of three hours and we never had anything to eat or drink during that whole period there.
And in my story, I usually say that we had more at Belsen than we had in Buckingham Palace kind of thing, but perhaps you ought to scrub that out.
And so we waited in this room.
And not even the lavatory paper had On Her Majesty's Service or anything on it.
It was just blank.
It was-- it was a big disappointment.
And we waited in this long room for ages and ages and ages.
And nothing happened.
What we hadn't realized, or I hadn't realized, not being very familiar with this kind of situation, was that the knights were done first and it was done in ever-decreasing order, obviously.
So Corinne and Judith and Nick saw everything, saw the knights being knighted and the other high-level people, Waldegrave and all kinds of people who were on that day being done.
And we were just standing in there waiting and waiting.
And nobody-- there was military people, because they get their military awards as well.
They were all talking to each other because they knew all the form.
But us other people, nobody knew anybody kind of thing and I couldn't recognize anybody.
I mean, Bruno, Frank Bruno, and who was the runner, the black runner?
Milt-- Linford Christie.
They all got MBEs that year, but they obviously were not in my lot.
There were about seven different investitures at fortnightly interval.
I mean, I would have recognized Linford Christie and Frank Bruno if they'd have been there, but I didn't.
And the England football captain, Bobby Robson, none of those were there.
There was nobody there that I recognized and I didn't talk to anybody.
And most of the others didn't talk to anybody.
We just stood there and waited and waited and waited.
And eventually, some lackey uniform came in.
And he said he would explain to us what we had to do when-- when the moment came.
And he demonstrated how we had to move in this direction, that direction-- no.
This direction, turn around, bow to the Queen, move forward to the Queen, then talk to the Queen.
When the Queen gave a handshake we had to retreat, bow, and turn that direction and move away.
So he demonstrated and he said, have you all understood that?
Nobody said a word.
So he said, I'll show it to you again.
So he showed it to us again.
He said, have you all understood that?
Nobody said a word.
He said, well, he said, if you forget, do what the person in front of you does and hope that he got it right.
And that was it.
So-- and then we carried on waiting and waiting and waiting.
And eventually a man came in and he called off about 15 names, not me.
And they went off and we were left there.
Then they called another 15 names and then another 15 names.
And now there was only about a dozen people left.
And we were just left there it seemed like hours.
And we were wondering whether we-- whether she hadn't got time for us or whether we'd come on the wrong day.
And we just waited.
But eventually this man came and he herded along corridors.
And we kept on being checked whether we had the right name and we were the right order, because otherwise everything would go wrong kind of thing.
And we had to be there exactly at the right second in front of Her Majesty the Queen kind of thing.
So eventually I got to the ballroom entrance door.
And then I was told, when your name is called, you move forward and you do your steps kind of thing.
So I heard the name called and I forgot everything else, because one puzzle was, why I got it.
And I never had anything in writing ever why I got it.
But it was announced Mr.
Paul Oppenheimer, MBE for services to industry.
My wife heard it, fortunately, because I didn't hear it.
I was totally-- as soon as I heard Oppenheimer, I kind of moved forward, bowed.
I did everything all right kind of thing, and I stood facing Her Majesty the Queen.
And she was on a podium kind of thing, so she was slightly above me.
And she shook my hand and said congratulations.
And she lobbed the medal onto me.
We'd all been equipped with a great big hook, so we all had a big plastic hook.
And she lobbed it onto this hook.
So there was no safety pins and blood and all this kind of stuff.
She lobbed it on there and then she said, what do you do?
And normally-- my wife always asked me this question.
And I tell people, my wife always asks me what I do because I've never really explained it to her.
But my wife told me not to say that.
And so I said to the Queen, I work on international standards and regulations.
And she said, how interesting.
Then I wanted to tell-- say about Belsen, you see.
But she got her hand out and shook my hand and that was a signal, you see.
It was very firm-handed.
It meant move kind of thing.
So I had to move backwards and bow and turn around.
But I got into a bit of a mess.
I think I turned around too early and Corinne says I didn't do it correctly compared to everybody else.
But anyway, I got out and they gave me the medal and I think that was the hub of it.
Nevertheless, looking back on it now, Belsen and Buckingham Palace have been the high points and the low points of my life.
Thank you, Paul.
You've said quite a lot of times that-- when I've said to you how did you feel about whatever, you said you didn't feel anything.
As a teenager, what do you think you do with these feelings?
I-- I just don't know.
I mean, people are always asking that question.
It was particularly noticeable when we went in 1990 to this first reunion in Belsen with Judith.
And this press man, he kept on asking me Judith every five minutes, how do you feel about this, how do you feel about that, how do you feel about that?
Judith was able to answer it.
But they asked me as well, but-- as you can see during the talk, there are one or two times when I get a bit stuck in the throat and maybe a bit emotional.
But generally I'm not a very-- well, that's what I like to think.
I just am like that.
I don't seem to be very emotional.
I don't seem to have a-- I can't express my feelings very well, but perhaps I do have them, you know.
I mean, I do get very upset.
I mean, when this Regina Franks, when we went to her funeral from-- Auschwitz survivor committed suicide, I was very emotionally upset about that.
I mean, I've been to funerals and things.
Yes, I do get upset.
But I-- I suppose I can't express my feelings very well.
I listened to a program the other day about astronauts when they're in the sky.
They're engineers and scientists and they're asked to express their feelings of what they see.
And all they say is they see a great big land mass and it's green and it's blue and it's yellow.
And people say, how do you feel?
And they say, well, I can see all this here kind of thing.
And they say they're not trained to express feelings.
We're practical engineers is my excuse.
And-- but I'm not saying that we don't have feelings.
But maybe we're not taught to express them.
And I'm very bad at words.
I mean, this is, again, where I never learned the English language properly.
I have a very small vocabulary, as you probably have noticed.
I don't use very many difficult, long, strange words.
And I perhaps have difficulty expressing my feelings.
It's possible.
I mean, people will read all sorts of things into the Holocaust, like this looking for souvenirs in Buckingham Palace, you see?
I will say we learnt it in the Holocaust.
But it's not really true.
Well, maybe it is true, but I would have learnt at a university or some other places just as much.
It's just a habit, especially Ritchie has always been a natural scavenger and I've learnt it from him kind of thing.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there's lots of things that-- that I do and I don't know and I don't do and I could say it was all due to the Holocaust.
Maybe it was.
How can you tell?
I don't know.
I mean, I'm-- I'm a bit of a loner.
And it's not until I've known anybody for quite a long time because I've become friendly with them.
And then I become very friendly with people.
I find it difficult to make friends on a quick basis kind of thing.
I could say my children are very different from me in that respect.

I think they're much more outgoing and extrovert than I am, although when I come on braking regulations and I give my talks everybody says I'm a great extrovert, because I keep mixing and jokes and all that sort of thing into braking regulations.
But I-- in my talks, I mix in jokes with the Holocaust.
And a lot of people think that's bad and a lot of people thinks that's my reaction to the Holocaust, that I joke about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You can say all these sort of thing.

And as I said to your colleague earlier on, if I went to a therapist, they could say all sorts of things, that I'm shut it away on purpose for 45 years, because I did nothing about the Holocaust for 45 years.
But it wasn't on purpose.
Nobody was interested.
If somebody would have wanted to talk about it, I was quite prepared to talk about it.
I was very busy in those years trying to catch up on studying and working and making a living and leading a normal life.
And I don't think I shut it away on purpose.
I just didn't find it interesting and I didn't think anybody else was very interested in the Holocaust.
